content.heading: Comparisons: Using yes or no questions to make decisions

blocks.text: **What are comparisons?** In the world of coding, computers aren't actually that smart—they are just really good at following rules! Every time a character jumps in a game or a door opens in a story, the computer is acting like a Robot Judge asking a simple question.

blocks.alt: Robot Judge with a speech bubble thinking about true/false
blocks.caption: The Robot Judge asks yes or no questions to determine what happens next.

blocks.text: The Robot Judge doesn't give long, rambling answers. It only knows two words: True (Yes!) or False (No!). We call these **Boolean** answers, but you can just think of them as a light switch that is either ON or OFF.

Comparisons are the building blocks of decision-making in code. They allow us to check if a player has enough points to win, if their guess matches the target, or if they have any lives left. By asking these yes/no questions, we can create dynamic and interactive experiences.

Sometimes we want to ask more complex questions that involve multiple conditions. For example, 'Is the player old enough AND do they have a ticket?' or 'Is the player a VIP OR do they have a coupon?' To do this, we can use logical operators to combine our comparisons.

blocks.desc: AND — both conditions must be true for the whole thing to be true
OR — at least one condition must be true
NOT — flips true to false and false to true

blocks.alt: Diagram showing how AND, OR, and NOT operators work.
blocks.caption: Logical operators let us combine multiple conditions into one question.

lines: // OR — at least one must be true

blocks.label: === not ==
blocks.text: Always use === (three equals signs) to check if things are equal. Using == (two equals signs) lets JavaScript change types before comparing, which causes bugs. Stick to === and you'll avoid a whole class of problems.

exercises.title: The Robot Judge's Answer
tasks: What is the result of the expression: 10 > 5?
exercises.explanation: Since 10 is indeed greater than 5, the comparison evaluates to true. In Simon Says, we might use this to check if a player's score has passed the high score.

exercises.title: Checking for Equality
exercises.hint: Remember that === checks both the value and the data type.
exercises.explanation: Even though they look like the same number, one is a 'number' and one is a 'string'. The strict equality operator (===) sees them as different.

exercises.title: The Not-Equal Operator
tasks: Which operator means 'is NOT equal to'?
exercises.hint: Look for the exclamation mark, which often means 'not' in coding.
exercises.explanation: !== is the 'strict inequality' operator. It's true if the values or types are different.

exercises.title: The Score Threshold
tasks: Complete the code to check if the score is 100 or more.
exercises.hint: You want the 'greater than or equal to' operator.
exercises.explanation: The >= operator checks if a value is greater than or exactly equal to the target. This is perfect for win conditions.

exercises.title: Match the Result
tasks: Sort these comparisons into the 'True' or 'False' buckets.
exercises.hint: Be careful with strings vs numbers and 'greater than' symbols.
exercises.explanation: Comparisons are like questions. 'Is 10 equal to 10?' Yes (true). 'Is 5 greater than 10?' No (false).

exercises.title: Simon Says: Color Match
tasks: Use the strict equality operator to check if the player's guess matches the target color.
exercises.hint: The operator is three equals signs in a row.
exercises.explanation: In Simon Says, we use === to confirm the user clicked the exact color the computer showed.

exercises.title: Order the logic
tasks: Order the lines to calculate if a player has run out of lives.
exercises.hint: First define the lives, then compare them to zero.
exercises.explanation: We check if lives are less than or equal to 0. If it's true, the game state changes to 'over'.

exercises.title: Strict vs Loose
exercises.hint: The '!==' operator checks if the value OR the type are different.
exercises.explanation: It is true that they are NOT strictly equal because a number is not the same type as a string.

exercises.title: Verify User Age
tasks: Set it to check if age is 13 or older.
exercises.explanation: Comparison operators allow our games to gate certain features or levels based on player data.

exercises.title: Is the String Empty?
tasks: Check if the player input is NOT an empty string.
exercises.hint: An empty string is written as ''. Use the not-equal operator.
exercises.explanation: Checking if input !== '' is a common way to make sure a player actually typed something in a prompt.

exercises.title: Level Up Logic
tasks: Check if the player's score is enough to reach Level 2 (200 points).

steps.text: Complete the assignment using the >= operator.

exercises.explanation: By comparing variables to thresholds, we can trigger level transitions in Simon Says.

exercises.title: The Boolean Switch
exercises.explanation: 10 is not greater than 20, so the 'Robot Judge' returns false.

exercises.title: Multi-type Comparison
tasks: Fix the comparison so it accurately checks if the number 5 is equal to the variable 'val'.
exercises.hint: val is a number, so use the strict equality operator.
exercises.explanation: When we want to check if two things are exactly the same, === is the standard tool.

exercises.title: Comparing Prompt Results
tasks: A user types '10' into a prompt. What is the result of (userInput === 10)?
exercises.hint: Think about the data type that prompt() returns.
exercises.explanation: prompt() returns a string ('10'), but we are comparing it to a number (10). Because they are different types, strict equality says false.

exercises.title: Final Truth Check
tasks: Fill in the blanks to verify if the player's name is NOT 'Guest'.
exercises.explanation: Using !== allows us to identify when a variable has changed from its default 'Guest' state.

challenges.title: Pass the Test
challenges.description: Simon is checking your score. You need exactly 50 points to pass this specific check. Fix the operator so the console logs 'true'.
challenges.hint: 50 is not GREATER than 50. Use an operator that checks if they are equal.
challenges.explanation: To check for an exact match, we use ===. Alternatively, >= would also work here, but === is more precise for an 'exactly 50' requirement.
